brief_justification: Arrives at the exact correct answer (Yes) with valid, multi-layered reasoning distinguishing referential scope from jargon density; traces second-order effects — what each abstraction level enables (specification evaluation vs. system debugging) — and third-order (Byzantine fault precision issue); explicitly names the negative gate risk by calling out how formal vocabulary 'can sound authoritative while subtly misfiring,' demonstrating active self-monitoring mid-analysis; applies a concrete falsification check: tests whether 'Byzantine fault' correctly characterizes a partition/omission failure vs. arbitrary-message failure, finding it likely imprecise; clearly hedges ('likely technically imprecise'), states what each perspective enables and limits, and identifies what observable difference (crash vs. conflicting messages) would resolve the imprecision; addresses the implicit competing interpretation ('equally technical therefore equivalent') and rejects it with term-level evidence, but does not enumerate alternative abstraction-classification frameworks; every conclusion traces to an explicit term-by-term table with concrete referents (named papers, algorithms, data structures) fully reproducible by a third party; explicitly invokes the negative gate by name when catching the Byzantine fault vocabulary drift, demonstrating awareness of the pattern; none of the suppressed failure modes appear — terms are grounded to concrete referents, no floating abstractions, no authoritative-stacking, and the agent's own injected context echoes the suppress list; reasoning follows the MONITORING_LOOP topology — decompose (C), apply grounding (S1-S2), interrupt and re-anchor on Byzantine fault (G1→INTERRUPT→S3), verify re-anchor (S4), and trace chain (S5), mapping closely to the graph structure.
